verses.text: Abraham takes another wife, Keturah, and has six more sons after Sarah's death. Their descendants populate Arabia and beyond. Even in old age, Abraham's life keeps multiplying — but none of these sons will carry the promise.

Abraham takes another wife, Keturah, and has six more sons after Sarah's death. Their descendants populate Arabia and beyond. Even in old age, Abraham's life keeps multiplying — but none of these sons will carry the promise.

Abraham takes another wife, Keturah, and has six more sons after Sarah's death. Their descendants populate Arabia and beyond. Even in old age, Abraham's life keeps multiplying — but none of these sons will carry the promise.

Abraham takes another wife, Keturah, and has six more sons after Sarah's death. Their descendants populate Arabia and beyond. Even in old age, Abraham's life keeps multiplying — but none of these sons will carry the promise.

Abraham gives everything to Isaac. To the sons of his concubines he gives gifts and sends them away east, while he is still alive. The inheritance is settled before death so there can be no rivalry around the grave.

Abraham gives everything to Isaac. To the sons of his concubines he gives gifts and sends them away east, while he is still alive. The inheritance is settled before death so there can be no rivalry around the grave.

Abraham lives 175 years and dies in a good old age, an old man, and full. The Hebrew word translated full means satisfied. He is gathered to his people — the first time this phrase is used in Scripture for someone leaving the body for a deeper home.

Abraham lives 175 years and dies in a good old age, an old man, and full. The Hebrew word translated full means satisfied. He is gathered to his people — the first time this phrase is used in Scripture for someone leaving the body for a deeper home.
Isaac and Ishmael bury him together. The two brothers, long separated, stand at the same grave. Death sometimes reunites what life couldn't keep together. They bury him in Machpelah, beside Sarah.
Isaac and Ishmael bury him together. The two brothers, long separated, stand at the same grave. Death sometimes reunites what life couldn't keep together. They bury him in Machpelah, beside Sarah.
After Abraham dies, God blesses Isaac. The covenant doesn't pause for a generation — it transfers immediately. Isaac settles by Beerlahairoi, the well where the angel once met Hagar.
Ishmael's generations. He had twelve sons — princes, just as God had promised Hagar at the well.
Ishmael's generations. He had twelve sons — princes, just as God had promised Hagar at the well.
The twelve are named — Nebajoth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadar, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — each one the founder of a tribe.
The twelve are named — Nebajoth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadar, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — each one the founder of a tribe.
The twelve are named — Nebajoth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadar, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — each one the founder of a tribe.
Ishmael lives 137 years, then is gathered to his people. His descendants settle from Havilah to Shur, near Egypt — at the edge of his kin. The line outside the covenant is still seen and named.
Ishmael lives 137 years, then is gathered to his people. His descendants settle from Havilah to Shur, near Egypt — at the edge of his kin. The line outside the covenant is still seen and named.

Now Isaac's generations. He marries Rebekah at forty. And like Sarah before her, Rebekah is barren. Isaac prays — entreats — for his wife. After twenty years, the LORD answers. The next generation also has to be prayed in.

Now Isaac's generations. He marries Rebekah at forty. And like Sarah before her, Rebekah is barren. Isaac prays — entreats — for his wife. After twenty years, the LORD answers. The next generation also has to be prayed in.

Now Isaac's generations. He marries Rebekah at forty. And like Sarah before her, Rebekah is barren. Isaac prays — entreats — for his wife. After twenty years, the LORD answers. The next generation also has to be prayed in.

The babies struggle inside her so violently that she goes to ask the LORD: why am I like this? God answers: two nations are in your womb. Two peoples will be separated from your body — and the elder shall serve the younger. The wrong child, by every cultural rule, will carry the line.

The babies struggle inside her so violently that she goes to ask the LORD: why am I like this? God answers: two nations are in your womb. Two peoples will be separated from your body — and the elder shall serve the younger. The wrong child, by every cultural rule, will carry the line.

Twins are born. The first is red and covered with hair — they call him Esau. The second comes out holding his brother's heel; they call him Jacob, meaning heel-grabber, or supplanter. Their names already tell their futures.

Twins are born. The first is red and covered with hair — they call him Esau. The second comes out holding his brother's heel; they call him Jacob, meaning heel-grabber, or supplanter. Their names already tell their futures.

Twins are born. The first is red and covered with hair — they call him Esau. The second comes out holding his brother's heel; they call him Jacob, meaning heel-grabber, or supplanter. Their names already tell their futures.

Esau grows up a cunning hunter, a man of the open country. Jacob is quiet and stays among the tents. Isaac loves Esau because he loves wild game. Rebekah loves Jacob. One marriage; two parents; two camps.

Esau grows up a cunning hunter, a man of the open country. Jacob is quiet and stays among the tents. Isaac loves Esau because he loves wild game. Rebekah loves Jacob. One marriage; two parents; two camps.

One day Jacob is cooking a red lentil stew, and Esau staggers in from the field, faint. Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red stew — that red, red. Hebrew has him sounding like a man too tired to find a noun. He is renamed Edom — meaning Red.

One day Jacob is cooking a red lentil stew, and Esau staggers in from the field, faint. Feed me, I pray thee, with that same red stew — that red, red. Hebrew has him sounding like a man too tired to find a noun. He is renamed Edom — meaning Red.
Jacob seizes the moment: sell me your birthright first. The firstborn's double portion and spiritual headship — Jacob has wanted them all his life. He offers a bowl of stew in trade.

I am about to die, says Esau — what use is a birthright to a dead man? Jacob makes him swear it. Esau swears, and his birthright is gone, traded for one meal. He thought it was nothing because he could not feel it.

I am about to die, says Esau — what use is a birthright to a dead man? Jacob makes him swear it. Esau swears, and his birthright is gone, traded for one meal. He thought it was nothing because he could not feel it.
Jacob gives him the bread and stew. Esau eats, drinks, rises, and walks away. Thus Esau despised his birthright — five short verbs name a lifetime of regret.
